table.table_description:
Summary
This table (intelliegencedatacatalog.bronze.raw_snr_data) is a raw/bronze-level ingestion of retail pharmaceutical sales and reference data. It captures individual outlet locations, product definitions (including standardized drug identifiers), transactional or period sales metrics, pricing, and metadata about the source files and load dates. The dataset is intended as the authoritative raw feed for downstream cleansing, enrichment, and analytic layers.

What the data represents (business view)
- Outlet/location details: where products are sold (store name, address, city/state/ZIP, outlet class/sub-class, territory and market identifiers). This supports analysis by geography, channel, or store type.
- Product and drug attributes: identifiers and descriptive fields for products (NDC_11, PRODUCT_ID, drug name/generic, manufacturer, strength, form, package size, unit of measure, USC/Broadband classification). These enable product-level rollups, mapping, and reconciliation.
- Pricing and financial fields: list prices and published benchmarks (WAC_DOLLARS, AWP) used to estimate revenues, margins, and price comparisons.
- Volume and sales measures: VOLUME_UNITS, PACK_UNITS, EXTENDED_UNITS and date fields (MONTH_ENDING_DATE, WEEK_ENDING_DATE) show quantity movement and time-period alignment for sales analytics.
- Regulatory/contract indicators: DEA_NUMBER and 340B_TRANSACTION_FLAG highlight controlled substances and transactions that may be subject to special pricing or reporting rules.
- Staging and provenance metadata: file name and file date columns for demos, products, and sales (e.g., DEMO_STAGED_FILE_NAME/DATE, PROD_STAGED_FILE_NAME/DATE, SALES_STAGED_FILE_NAME/DATE) plus REL_ID which appears to act as the raw record identifier. These support data lineage and troubleshooting.

Key column groups (examples)
- Identification: REL_ID, PRODUCT_ID, MARKET_ID, TERRITORY_ID
- Outlet info: OUTLET_NAME, ADDRESS, CITY, STATE, ZIP_CODE, OUTLET_CLASS_OF_TRADE, OUTLET_SUB_CLASS_OF_TRADE
- Product/drug: NDC_11, DRUG_NAME, DRUG_GENERIC_NAME, MANUFACTURER, FORM_CODE/FORM_DESCRIPTION, STRENGTH_DESCRIPTION, PACKAGE_SIZE, UNIT_OF_MEASURE
- Classification: USC_CODE/USC_DESCRIPTION, BB_USC_CODE/BB_USC_DESCRIPTION
- Pricing & financials: WAC_DOLLARS, AWP
- Volume & sales: VOLUME_UNITS, PACK_UNITS, EXTENDED_UNITS, MONTH_ENDING_DATE, WEEK_ENDING_DATE
- Regulatory: DEA_NUMBER, 340B_TRANSACTION_FLAG
- Provenance: *_STAGED_FILE_NAME, *_STAGED_FILE_DATE

Common business uses
- Sales and volume trend analysis by product, market, or outlet
- Price benchmarking and margin analysis using WAC/AWP
- Product mapping and NDC-level reconciliation
- Regulatory and compliance reporting (340B, controlled substances)
- Feeding cleaned, enriched datasets into Silver/Gold layers for dashboards, forecasting, and commercial planning

Notes and considerations
- As a bronze/raw table, fields are likely untransformed and may contain duplicates, inconsistent formats, or missing values; downstream cleansing is expected before production analytics.
- Contains sensitive location and regulatory identifiers (addresses, DEA_NUMBER); handle with appropriate access controls and data governance.

columns.description:
Business description:
REL_ID is the numeric identifier that links each raw SNR record to its related business entity/relationship (for example a device, customer relationship, or parent record in the relational master table). It is used as a surrogate/foreign key to join SNR measurements to the corresponding entity and to track history for that entity.

Expected format:
- Integer (LONG) — whole number, typically positive (0 or greater).
- Stored as a 64-bit integer with no punctuation, leading zeros, or decimal places (e.g., 12345, 9876543210).
- Intended to match the REL_ID values in the corresponding master table; may be null only when a record is not yet linked.

Business description
- Meaning: The outlet’s trade channel classification — a single categorical label that identifies the type of retail or distribution outlet where the sale or measurement was captured (the commercial “class of trade” for that outlet). Used for segmentation, reporting, pricing and channel performance analysis.

Expected format and rules
- Data type: STRING; one canonical category per record (not a multi-value field). Nulls allowed when unknown.
- Controlled vocabulary: values should come from a predefined list of trade-channel categories (standardized across the catalog). Examples: Grocery (Supermarket), Drug/Pharmacy, Convenience (C‑store), Mass Merchant, Club, Specialty/Retailer, Dollar/Discount, E‑commerce / Online, Foodservice / On‑Premise, Direct‑to‑Consumer.
- Formatting conventions: use the canonical category name exactly (recommended title case or UPPERCASE per org standard), avoid free-text descriptions, abbreviations, or embedded location/store IDs. Limit length (e.g., ≤ 50 chars) and avoid punctuation/special characters.
- Validation/mapping: values should be mapped to and validated against a master list of classes of trade; map synonyms (e.g., “Supermarket” → “Grocery”) to the canonical term.

Example values
- "Grocery"
- "Drug/Pharmacy"
- "Convenience"
- "Mass Merchant"
- "E‑commerce / Online"

Definition
- The OUTLET_SUB_CLASS_OF_TRADE field identifies the detailed sub-channel (sub-class) of the retail or distribution outlet where a transaction or sensor/read event occurred. It refines the broader outlet class-of-trade (e.g., Grocery, Pharmacy) into more specific trading formats used for segmentation and reporting.

Business purpose
- Enables finer-grained analysis of sales, distribution, pricing and channel performance (e.g., distinguishing national supermarket vs. independent grocery vs. discount retailer vs. online marketplace).

Expected format
- Data type: STRING (categorical).
- Controlled vocabulary: single categorical label drawn from a predefined list or lookup table (recommended).
- Normalization: uppercase or title-case consistent across records, trimmed of leading/trailing whitespace, no embedded control characters.
- Length: short text (recommend <= 50 characters); avoid long free-text descriptions.

Examples (typical values)
- SUPERMARKET - NATIONAL
- SUPERMARKET - REGIONAL
- DISCOUNT RETAILER
- CONVENIENCE - FORECOURT
- CONVENIENCE - CORNER STORE
- INDEPENDENT GROCERY
- WHOLESALE CLUB
- PHARMACY / DRUGSTORE
- SPECIALTY FOOD STORE
- ONLINE MARKETPLACE
- FOOD SERVICE - QUICK SERVICE
- OTHER / UNKNOWN

Data-quality rules & recommendations
- Validate against an authoritative lookup table; map legacy or free-text variants to canonical values.
- Use a fixed value for unknown/missing (e.g., "UNKNOWN" or "OTHER") rather than NULL where business logic requires grouping.
- Trim whitespace and standardize case on ingestion.
- Maintain a reference table linking OUTLET_SUB_CLASS_OF_TRADE to OUTLET_CLASS_OF_TRADE for hierarchical reporting.

Notes
- If multiple sub-classes could apply, store the primary sub-class here and use a related table for additional attributes.

Business description
- OUTLET_NAME is the human‑readable identifier of the physical or logical outlet that originated the raw SNR record (e.g., a retail store, warehouse, distribution point, service center or sales channel). It ties the data row to the specific location or business unit responsible for the transaction or measurement.

Expected format and rules
- Stored as a STRING; value should use the canonical name from the company’s outlet/master-location registry when available.
- Typical formats accepted:
  - "OutletCode - OutletName" (e.g., "12345 - Downtown Store")
  - "OutletName, City" (e.g., "Main Street Pharmacy, Seattle")
  - Short codes for automated systems (e.g., "WH_AZ_02" or "POPUP_LA01")
- Characters: letters, digits, spaces, hyphens, underscores, slashes and parentheses are allowed; avoid non‑printable characters and excessive punctuation.
- Case: case-insensitive (store canonical capitalization where possible).
- Uniqueness: not required to be globally unique in this column alone — use OutletCode or master key for uniqueness if needed.
- Null/unknown: use a standardized token such as "UNKNOWN" or NULL when the outlet is not determined.

Examples
- "12345 - Downtown Store"
- "Warehouse A - Boston"
- "POPUP_LA01"
- "UNKNOWN"

Business description
- The ADDRESS column contains the human-readable postal address for the entity represented by the record (e.g., sensor/site/customer location, installation address, or event location). It is used for display, routing, geocoding, location matching and downstream analytics.

Expected format and content
- Free‑form single string representing a postal address: typically street number + street name + unit/designator (optional) + city/locality + state/province + postal/zip code + country (optional).  
- Examples:
  - "123 Main St, Apt 4B, Springfield, IL 62704, USA"
  - "PO Box 456, Vancouver, BC V6B 3K9, Canada"
  - "10 Downing St, Westminster, London SW1A 2AA, UK"
- Acceptable characters: letters, digits, punctuation and common address symbols (#, -, /, , .).

Notes and recommendations
- This is raw/free‑text data and may contain inconsistent abbreviations, missing components, or formatting differences by country. For reliable geocoding and matching, normalize to a standard postal format (e.g., USPS/Canada Post rules) or split into structured fields (street, unit, city, state/province, postal_code, country).  
- Treat as potentially sensitive PII and apply appropriate access controls and masking where required.  
- Recommended validation: trim whitespace, enforce a reasonable max length (e.g., 255 chars), and flag clearly invalid values (empty strings, placeholders like "N/A").

Column name: CITY

Business meaning:
- The human-readable name of the city associated with the raw SNR observation or the device/location that produced the record. Used for reporting, grouping, geospatial joins and filtering.

Expected format and rules:
- STRING containing a single city name, optionally followed by administrative subdivision separated by a comma (e.g., "Austin" or "Austin, TX").
- Use standardized, canonical names in Title Case (e.g., "San Francisco", not "san francisco" or "SF").
- Avoid postal codes, GPS coordinates or country-only values; include country only when necessary to disambiguate (e.g., "Paris, France").
- Trim leading/trailing whitespace; do not include trailing punctuation.
- Null/empty allowed if city is unknown.
- Suggested max length: 100 characters.

Examples: "New York", "Munich", "São Paulo", "Sydney, NSW"

Business description
- Meaning: Geographic U.S. state or territory associated with the record (where the event/data point originated or is applicable).
- Expected format: two‑letter USPS state/territory postal abbreviation in uppercase (e.g., CA, NY, TX, DC, PR). Stored as a STRING with no leading/trailing spaces or punctuation.
- Allowed values: the 50 U.S. states plus DC and common territories (PR, VI, GU, AS, MP). Use standard USPS codes only.
- Nulls/unknowns: prefer NULL for missing values; if a placeholder is used, standardize to a single token (e.g., "UNKNOWN") and document it.
- Validation/cleanup guidance: trim whitespace, convert to uppercase, map full state names to USPS codes, and reject or flag non‑standard values.

Meaning
- ZIP_CODE is the postal (ZIP) code identifying the customer or address delivery area (U.S. postal code used for geolocation, routing, reporting and demographic joins).

Expected format and notes
- Although stored as a LONG (integer), this field represents a 5‑digit categorical postal code. Expected logical format is a 5‑digit string (e.g., "02115", "90210"). 
- Because numeric storage drops leading zeros, values may appear as 2115 instead of 02115; consumers should zero‑pad to 5 digits for display and matching.
- If ZIP+4 values are required, they should be stored in a separate field or as a zero‑padded 9‑digit string (no hyphen) rather than relying on the LONG type.
- Valid range: 00001–99999 (practically limited to assigned U.S. ZIP codes); allow NULL/unknown for missing addresses.

Recommendation
- Treat ZIP_CODE as a categorical string in downstream processes and validate/format by zero‑padding to 5 digits for display and joins.

Business description:
ZIP_SECTOR is a numeric identifier that groups records by postal delivery sector derived from the address ZIP code — used for geographic aggregation, routing, service-area analysis, and join-keying to postal/demographic reference data.

Expected format:
- Stored as a numeric (DOUBLE) but represents an integer postal code fragment (no fractional meaning).
- Typical values are 3–5 digit ZIP-derived codes (e.g., 021, 02134) or a sector-level code derived from a ZIP+4; examples: 123, 94103, 10001.
- Because the column is numeric, leading zeros may be lost (e.g., 021 -> 21). There should be no decimal portions (e.g., 94103.0 is acceptable but .0 carries no meaning).

Data quality rules / recommendations:
- Values must be non-negative and contain only digits (no embedded punctuation).
- If leading zeros are significant, store or present the value as a zero-padded string of fixed width (3 or 5 chars as appropriate) or maintain an integer with metadata describing width.
- Validate against a reference ZIP/sector table where possible and allow nulls for unknown/missing postal data.

Meaning
- The DEA_NUMBER is the Drug Enforcement Administration registration identifier assigned to a U.S. registrant (individual practitioner, hospital, pharmacy, manufacturer, distributor, etc.) that is authorized to handle, prescribe, dispense or manufacture controlled substances. It is used to uniquely identify the registrant in regulatory, prescribing and claims contexts.

Expected format
- Canonical: a 9-character string with two letters followed by seven digits (e.g., AB1234563). Regular expression: ^[A-Z]{2}\d{7}$.
- Variants commonly seen in raw data: lowercase letters, embedded hyphens or spaces (e.g., AB-1234563 or ab 1234563). Normalize by removing non-alphanumerics and uppercasing.
- Notes: some registrants may have the special “X” prefix for opioid treatment waivers (e.g., XJ1234567). The final digit is a check digit that can be used to validate the number. Nulls, malformed values or non‑U.S. identifiers may appear and should be flagged in quality checks.

Meaning
- TERRITORY_ID is the canonical identifier that ties each raw_snr_data row to a business/geographic territory used for reporting, routing and aggregation (e.g., country, sales territory, operational region).

Expected format
- A short, standardized string code (not a free-text name) that maps to the organization's territory master list.
- Common forms you will see:
  - ISO country code: "US", "GB"
  - Hierarchical region code: "US-CA", "EU-FR"
  - Internal numeric or alphanumeric ID: "T00123" or "10045"
- Validation guidelines (recommended): uppercase, alphanumeric with optional hyphen or underscore, no spaces, and typically limited to ~20 characters.
- NOTE: Values should be resolved against the enterprise territory dimension/master table; use NULL or a special token (e.g., "UNKNOWN") only when territory cannot be determined.

Meaning
- The original staged source filename (or staging path+filename) from which each raw SNR record was ingested. Used for traceability, lineage, audit, replay and debugging of ingestion batches.

Expected format
- A filename including extension, optionally prefixed by the staging location/path. Recommended, human- and machine-friendly pattern:
  <source>_<feed>_<YYYYMMDD>_<HHMMSS>_<batchId>.<ext>
  - source/feed: alphanumeric, dashes or underscores
  - YYYYMMDD: eight-digit date
  - HHMMSS: six-digit time
  - batchId: short numeric or alphanumeric identifier
  - ext: csv | json | parquet | txt, etc.

Examples
- payments_SNR_20230228_153012_001.csv
- s3://staging/appA/ingest/user_events_20250801_000001.json

Guidelines
- Avoid spaces and special characters; max length 255 characters recommended.
- Must be populated for each ingestion so records can be traced back to the original file.

Meaning: The calendar date associated with the staged demo/source file that produced this raw record — i.e., the file’s business or file‑generation date (the date the data in the staged file pertains to), not the ingestion or processing timestamp.

Expected format: A DATE value in ISO 8601 date-only form: YYYY-MM-DD (e.g., 2024-02-28). No time or timezone component. Null values indicate the file date was not provided.

Meaning
- MARKET_ID is the numeric identifier for the commercial/operational market (geographic region, country, trading venue, or business market unit) where the raw SNR measurement or record was captured. It is used to attribute records to a specific market and to join this raw data to master market metadata.

Expected format
- Integer (LONG): non-negative whole number, no decimals. Stored as a numeric (e.g., 123, 1001, 20045).
- No leading zeros (i.e., 00123 should be stored as 123).
- Value must correspond to the canonical Market table/key in the enterprise master data (referential integrity).

Business rules / validation
- Required for most analytical uses (should be NOT NULL where market is known); if market is unknown, use a documented sentinel (e.g., NULL or 0) per data governance policy.
- Must map to an existing MARKET_ID in the markets reference dimension; otherwise flag as invalid for downstream processing.
- Use as join key for enrichment (market name, region, timezone, regulatory attributes).

Examples
- 1 (Global), 101 (North America), 2503 (EMEA–Market 3) — actual codes follow the organization’s market-code dictionary.

Purpose / meaning
- Identifies the market, region, or trading venue to which the raw SNR (signal/noise) record applies. Used to group, filter and join SNR measurements to market-level metadata, reporting and downstream analytics.

Expected content and examples
- A human- and machine-readable market identifier such as a country, region, exchange, or market segment.
- Examples: "United States", "US", "US-NY", "NASDAQ", "LSE", "EMEA", "APAC", "Germany", "DEU", "Tokyo".

Recommended format and validation
- STRING (already defined). Store a single canonical identifier per row.
- Prefer standardized codes where available (ISO country codes like "US" or "DEU", exchange tickers like "NASDAQ") or a documented canonical name (e.g., "United States" vs "US").
- Trim leading/trailing whitespace; use consistent casing (recommend: UPPERCASE for codes, Title Case for names).
- Avoid embedded control characters; limit length (suggest <= 100 characters).

Nulls and defaults
- Should not be ambiguous; if market is unknown, use a consistent sentinel such as "UNKNOWN" or NULL per organizational standards.

Business notes
- Consumers expect MARKET_NAME to be the key for joining to market master data (fees, timezone, currency). Keep a mapping table of canonical values to ensure consistency across sources.

Business description
- PRODUCT_ID is the numeric identifier that links each raw SNR record to a specific product (device, sensor, or SKU) in the product/master catalog. It is used to join this raw measurement data to product metadata (name, model, vendor, configuration).

Expected format and rules
- Data type: 64-bit integer (LONG).  
- Values: non‑fractional whole numbers (positive integers). Example values: 1001, 200345.  
- Semantics: acts as a reference/foreign key to the product registry; not necessarily unique in this table (multiple SNR measurements can share the same PRODUCT_ID).  
- Missing/unknown handling: should be null or use a documented sentinel (e.g., -1) if no product mapping exists — avoid storing zeros with ambiguous meaning.  
- Validation: must match an entry in the master product table when present; reject non-integer, negative (unless sentinel) or out-of-range values.

Business description
- PROD_PRODUCT_NAME identifies the product associated with the raw SNR record. At the business level it represents the product sold, installed or serviced and is used to join SNR records to the enterprise product/master catalog for reporting, billing and entitlement checks.

Expected format and rules
- Logical content: a canonical product identifier (either a human‑readable product name or a numeric product code depending on source).
- If textual: an alphanumeric product name (UTF‑8), e.g. "Acme Router X1000", "Residential Internet — 100 Mbps". Prefer normalized casing and no leading/trailing whitespace.
- If numeric (LONG used as integer id): a non‑negative integer product_id (e.g. 10023456) that must map to the master product table.
- Nulls/unknowns: empty, zero or null values indicate unknown product and should be treated as unmapped until resolved.
- Integration rule: values in this column must map to the canonical product master (ID → name) during bronze→silver transformation; trim whitespace and normalize common synonyms before mapping.

Meaning
- NDC_11 is the normalized 11‑digit National Drug Code for a specific drug product/packaging. It uniquely identifies the labeler, product, and package for a pharmaceutical item.

Expected format
- Exactly 11 numeric digits, zero‑padded as necessary (no dashes or letters). Typical segmentation is 5-4-2 (labeler–product–package). Examples: 01234567890, 12345678901.

Business rules / validation
- Must be an integer value (no decimals) and 11 digits long.
- Treat as an identifier, not a numeric quantity — do not perform arithmetic.
- Because the column is stored as DOUBLE, beware loss of leading zeros or conversion to scientific notation; when extracting or displaying, convert/preserve as an 11‑character string padded with leading zeros.

Recommendation
- For downstream use (joins, lookups, reporting) cast/convert to a fixed-length string of 11 characters to ensure consistent comparisons and presentation.

USC_CODE — Business meaning
- A numeric source identifier assigned by the originating system that uniquely identifies the entity referenced by each raw SNR record (e.g., a customer, subscriber, security or other subject of the SNR measurement). It is used for lookups, joins and record-level deduplication across systems.

Expected format
- Integer (LONG) value; positive whole number, no decimals or punctuation.  
- Treated as an opaque identifier (do not parse for semantic parts).  
- Must be unique per source-entity (or combined with a source/system id if the same numeric space is reused).  
- Typical examples: 12345, 1000001.  
- If absent in source data, a surrogate key should be created and documented.

Business description
- USC_DESCRIPTION is the human‑readable description of the USC value associated with a raw SNR record. It explains in plain language what the record refers to (the item, component, issue, service or condition referenced by the USC code) so business users can understand the SNR without looking up codes.

Expected format
- Type: free‑text string (UTF‑8).
- Typical length: short summary — recommended ≤ 255 characters (system may allow longer raw text).
- Content: plain text (no HTML or binary), may include common abbreviations, numbers and punctuation; avoid control characters and embedded newlines.
- Nullability: optional in raw bronze, but should be populated when available; downstream processing should normalize (trim whitespace, unify casing or canonical abbreviations).
- Examples: "Pressure sensor failure, valve A12", "Cable assembly, coax 10m", "Leak at pump #3 — seal worn".

Data quality recommendations
- Trim leading/trailing whitespace, remove embedded control characters.
- Prefer standardized terminology/abbreviations to support downstream grouping and search.

Business description:
- BB_USC_CODE is an opaque numeric identifier (LONG) that represents the dataset’s U.S.-centric classification code for the row (e.g., a Bloomberg-assigned US classification, security/industry class, or statutory category).  
- It is intended to be used as a key to join against a reference/lookup table that maps the numeric code to a human-readable category and metadata.

Expected format and constraints:
- Data type: 64-bit integer (LONG).  
- Value: non‑decimal, positive integer (no leading zeros); absent values may be NULL.  
- Example: 12345.

Business definition
- Human-readable description of the U.S. security / instrument associated with the record (the descriptive label supplied by the upstream source). This field is meant to explain what the record represents (issuer name, instrument type/class, and commonly one or more identifiers such as ticker, CUSIP or maturity).

Expected format and usage
- Free‑form text (STRING). Typically a short phrase containing issuer + instrument details (e.g., issuer name, security type, maturity or ticker).
- No strict schema — do not treat this as a unique identifier. Intended for display, filtering and human interpretation rather than deterministic parsing.
- Recommended limits/constraints: trimmed text, up to ~255 characters, UTF‑8, allow punctuation, upper/lower case. Empty/null allowed when source provides no description.

Examples
- "APPLE INC COM"
- "US TREASURY 2.5% 11/15/2025"
- "AAPL US EQUITY"

Business description
- DRUG_NAME contains the drug or medicinal product name as recorded in the raw source feed for the SNR dataset. It represents the therapy reported in an event/record and is captured in the source’s original free-text form (i.e., the bronze/raw value).

Expected format and notes
- Data type: STRING (free text). May be null if no drug was reported.
- Typical contents: generic name or brand name, sometimes with strength, dosage form, route, or manufacturer (e.g., "ibuprofen 200 mg tablet", "Tylenol PM", "metformin ER 500 mg", "amoxicillin/clavulanate").
- Multiple active ingredients may appear in one field (separated by “/”, “+”, or commas).
- Common issues: spelling variants, abbreviations, case differences, stray punctuation, and embedded dosing information.
- Recommended downstream handling: keep raw value unchanged in this bronze layer; for analytics or coding, normalize/match to a standard vocabulary (RxNorm, ATC, or an internal drug master) and parse out strength, form, and route into separate fields.

Business description
- DRUG_GENERIC_NAME contains the non‑proprietary (generic) name of the active pharmaceutical ingredient(s) reported for a signal/noise/raw safety record. It identifies the substance(s) responsible for the therapeutic effect, not the brand/trade name or packaging.

Expected format and validation guidance
- Type: free‑text string (recommended max length 255).
- Content: generic/international nonproprietary name (INN/USAN/RxNorm preferred). Include salt or salt form when routinely part of the generic name (e.g., "metformin hydrochloride"); for combination products use a delimiter (e.g., "amoxicillin/clavulanate").
- Do not include dose, strength, route, package size or units (e.g., omit "500 mg", "tablet", "oral").
- Avoid brand/trade names; map brand names to the corresponding generic.
- Character rules: letters, numbers (for some salts), spaces, hyphens, slashes and parentheses allowed; trim leading/trailing whitespace.
- Null/unknown: allowed if generic cannot be determined; flag for enrichment.
- Normalization: map to a standard vocabulary (RxNorm or ATC codes) where possible to support de‑duplication and analytics.

Examples
- Valid: "ibuprofen", "metformin hydrochloride", "amoxicillin/clavulanate"
- Invalid: "Tylenol 500 mg tablet", "Aspirin - 81 mg", "Lipitor" (brand)

Use these rules to drive ingestion, de‑duplication, and linkage to drug reference tables.

Business description
- FORM_CODE is the canonical identifier for the source form or template that produced this raw SNR record. It ties each row back to the originating questionnaire/form/version so downstream teams can look up form metadata (friendly name, owner, effective date, field definitions), apply the correct validation/transform rules, and support auditing and lineage.

Expected format and validation rules
- Data type: STRING.
- Typical pattern: a short, machine-friendly code (alphanumeric, no spaces), often upper-case, optionally using hyphens or underscores to separate logical parts (e.g., source + version).
- Recommended regex: ^[A-Z0-9_-]{1,30}$ (trim whitespace before validation).
- Examples: SNR-01, FORM_A, F1234, CUSTOMER_V2.
- Business rules: must map to the master forms reference table; treat empty or unknown values as "UNKNOWN" or NULL only after verification; normalize to upper-case and trimmed form during ingestion.

If you want, I can propose a stricter pattern or a list of accepted FORM_CODE values based on the existing master form registry.

Business description
- Human-readable label that identifies and summarizes the purpose or type of the source form associated with each raw SNR data record (for example the survey, report, intake form or instrument that produced the row).
- Used for reporting, grouping, filtering and joining raw records to downstream form metadata or master reference tables.

Expected format and constraints
- TEXT string (UTF-8). Preferably a short descriptive phrase (recommended max 200–255 characters).
- Plain text, no HTML or embedded control characters; single-line (trim leading/trailing whitespace, avoid internal newlines).
- Prefer standardized values where possible (controlled vocabulary / lookup key) — e.g., "Customer Intake Form", "Field Observation Sheet", "Monthly SNR Survey", "Incident Report".
- Should not contain sensitive personal data (PII) or encoded binary content.

Quality checks / recommendations
- Validate against a reference list of known form descriptions and map variants to canonical names.
- Mark as NULL only when no form applies; otherwise populate with a non-empty string.
- Normalize casing and spelling during ingestion (e.g., Title Case) to support consistent reporting.

Short business description
- Human-readable, qualitative description of the signal strength associated with an SNR (signal‑to‑noise ratio) record. Used for filtering, grouping and user-facing reports where a textual strength label is preferable to raw numeric SNR values.

Expected format and constraints
- Type: short categorical text (STRING)
- Typical values: a small, fixed set such as "Very Weak", "Weak", "Moderate", "Strong", "Very Strong", "Unknown" or "Not Available"
- Presentation: single phrase, title case preferred, no trailing punctuation, no embedded numeric ranges (keep purely descriptive)
- Length: should be short (recommend <= 50 characters)
- Nullability: may be NULL/empty if strength cannot be determined; use "Unknown" to indicate explicit unknowns
- Normalization: values should be normalized to a standard set on ingestion (trim whitespace, map synonyms like "Poor"→"Weak", "N/A"→"Unknown")

Optional mapping guidance (for implementation)
- If numeric SNR is available, map to categories consistently (example thresholds):
  - Very Weak: SNR < 5 dB
  - Weak: 5 ≤ SNR < 10 dB
  - Moderate: 10 ≤ SNR < 20 dB
  - Strong: 20 ≤ SNR < 30 dB
  - Very Strong: SNR ≥ 30 dB

Notes
- This column is intended for business readability; authoritative numeric analysis should use the raw SNR numeric column where available. Ensure upstream normalization rules are documented so analytics and dashboards show consistent labels.

Business description
- PACKAGE_SIZE is the numeric magnitude of a product’s packaged quantity (size, weight, volume, or count) recorded for each raw SNR record. It represents how large or how many items are in the package and is used for pricing, logistics, inventory and analytics.

Expected format and rules
- Data type: DOUBLE (decimal numeric).
- Meaning of value: the numeric measure only — the unit of measure must be interpreted from the companion unit field (e.g., PACKAGE_UNIT or PACKAGE_UOM) or dataset-level metadata. The column by itself does not encode units.
- Allowed values: non-negative numbers; use NULL when package size is unknown. Do not use negative values. Prefer NULL rather than 0 for unknown; 0 is allowed only if the business meaning is truly zero.
- Precision: decimals allowed (e.g., 0.25, 250.0).
- Typical examples: 250.0 (250 grams if PACKAGE_UNIT = "g"), 1.5 (1.5 liters if PACKAGE_UNIT = "L"), 12.0 (12 units if PACKAGE_UNIT = "each").

Quality checks to enforce
- Validate PACKAGE_SIZE >= 0 or NULL.
- Ensure a unit field is present and populated for proper interpretation of the numeric value.
- Flag outliers (unreasonably large or small values) for review.

Column name: MANUFACTURER

Purpose
- Identifies the company that manufactured the sensor or device which produced the SNR (signal‑to‑noise ratio) measurement. Used for filtering, grouping, vendor reporting and device compatibility analyses.

What the value means
- A human‑readable vendor name (brand/OEM) for the device that generated the raw SNR record.

Expected format and examples
- Free‑text company name, ideally normalized to a canonical form.
- Examples: "Siemens", "Honeywell", "Samsung", "Qualcomm", "Bosch", "Unknown"
- Recommended maximum length: 100 characters.
- Character set: printable ASCII/UTF‑8 letters, numbers and common punctuation (avoid control characters).

Data quality / normalization guidance
- Normalize to a canonical master list of manufacturers (use an external lookup table or manufacturer_id mapping).
- Trim leading/trailing whitespace; collapse duplicate whitespace; remove accidental control chars.
- Standardize casing (e.g., Title Case or UPPER for keys) and remove common legal suffixes if desired (e.g., "Inc.", "Ltd.", "Corp.") only if consistent with business rules.
- Map common aliases and misspellings to the canonical name (e.g., "Hewlett Packard" → "HP", "Siemmens" → "Siemens").
- Use a consistent sentinel for unknown/unspecified values (e.g., NULL or "UNKNOWN").

Validation rules to enforce
- Not contain solely numeric values unless that numeric string is the canonical manufacturer identifier.
- Enforce membership against the canonical manufacturer list for production/curated datasets.
- Flag unusual/rare values for review (low frequency or new unknown vendors).

Downstream usage notes
- Prefer joining against a manufacturer master table with manufacturer_id, canonical_name, country, and support/contact info for accurate reporting and deduplication.

Description
- UNIT_OF_MEASURE is the human- and machine-readable label for the physical unit in which the recorded measurement value is expressed (e.g., length, temperature, concentration, signal level).
- It explains how to interpret the numeric measurement in the same row (VALUE, MEASUREMENT, READING, etc.).

Expected format and rules
- Type: short string containing a unit symbol or standardized token (not a numeric value or conversion factor).
- Preferred: use standard unit symbols from a recognized system (SI/UCUM). Examples: "m", "cm", "kg", "s", "V", "mV", "µV", "°C", "K", "ppm", "dB", "%", "counts", "unitless".
- If Unicode is problematic, use ASCII equivalents: "uV" for "µV", "degC" or "C" for "°C" (document the convention).
- Case and formatting: keep consistent canonical form (prefer lower/upper as per chosen standard), no surrounding whitespace, no pluralization (use "m", not "meters").
- Null/unknown: use a fixed token such as NULL or "UNKNOWN" when unit is not available; prefer explicit "unitless" for dimensionless quantities.
- Validation: unit must be semantically consistent with related columns (e.g., MEASUREMENT_TYPE); map incoming variants to canonical units during ingestion.

Examples
- Valid: "dB", "m", "m/s", "V", "ppm", "unitless"
- Invalid: "meters per second" (use "m/s"), "deg C " (trailing space), "10 m" (contains a numeric value)

Business description
- PROD_STAGED_FILE_NAME contains the original filename (or staged file path) of the product-level source file as it landed in the ingestion/staging area. It provides traceability between a raw SNR data record and the physical file that produced it, for auditing, reprocessing, and troubleshooting.

Expected content and format
- A UTF-8 string representing the file name or full staged path and must include the file extension.
- Preferred canonical pattern (recommended): <PRODUCT_CODE>_<SOURCE>_<YYYYMMDD>T<HHMMSS>Z.<ext>
  - Examples:
    - PROD123_SNR_20260301T153005Z.csv
    - PROD123_SNR_20260301T153005Z.csv.gz
    - s3://bucket/staging/prod/PROD123_SNR_20260301T153005Z.json
- Common extensions: .csv, .json, .xml, .parquet and compressed variants (.gz, .zip).
- Constraints / validation guidance:
  - No spaces; use underscores or dashes.
  - Use ISO-8601 compact timestamp (YYYYMMDDTHHMMSSZ) for uniqueness and sortability.
  - Limit length to reasonable size (e.g., <= 255 chars) and avoid non-printable/special characters.
  - Must be unique per staged file within the same ingest batch.

Business usage
- Used for lineage, file retrieval from staging, duplicate detection, and linking raw records back to their source file for reprocessing or audit.

Meaning
- The date on which a production data file was staged (placed into the landing/ingestion area) for downstream processing. This is a business-facing provenance field used to track when the source file became available to the pipeline (not the date it was processed in the warehouse).

Expected format and constraints
- Stored as a DATE (calendar date, no time or timezone).
- Use ISO format: YYYY-MM-DD (example: 2026-03-13).
- Valid values: any valid calendar date; should not be null when file staging date is known and should not be in the future.
- Source of value: extracted from file metadata/header or naming convention (not inferred from DB load timestamp).
- Typical uses: lineage, partitioning, incremental ingestion, deduplication and retention policies.

Business description
- Meaning: The MONTH_ENDING_DATE identifies the reporting month for each record by giving the month’s closing date — i.e., the last calendar day of the applicable reporting month. It is used to group, filter and trend data on a monthly basis.

Expected format (stored as LONG)
- Canonical representation: an integer in YYYYMMDD form (for example, 20231231 for December 31, 2023).
- Business rules: value must correspond to the actual month-end (28/29/30/31 depending on month and leap year). Nulls should indicate no applicable reporting month.
- Notes: If your environment uses Unix epoch values instead, store epoch milliseconds (long) and document that convention; however YYYYMMDD as a numeric LONG is recommended for readability and easy month-level grouping.

Business description
- Indicates whether the transaction (claim/dispensing/sale) is associated with the federal 340B Drug Pricing Program. A true/positive flag means the drug/transaction was processed or billed as a 340B-covered transaction (e.g., dispensed to a 340B-covered entity or through a contract pharmacy).

Expected format and values
- Stored as a short string (typically length 1).
- Common/expected values:
  - "Y" / "N" (preferred)
  - or equivalent: "YES"/"NO", "1"/"0", "TRUE"/"FALSE" (case-insensitive)
- Null or empty = unknown / not recorded / not applicable.

Notes and usage
- Used for revenue, billing, compliance and reporting logic (e.g., reconciliation with 340B entitlement lists, rebate/discount tracking).
- Normalize to a boolean in analytics (Y/YES/1/TRUE -> true; N/NO/0/FALSE -> false). Treat nulls as unknown and validate against provider enrollment where possible — a flag alone does not guarantee program eligibility or compliance.

Business description
- WAC_DOLLARS is the Wholesale Acquisition Cost (WAC) per unit for the product in the raw_snr_data feed. It represents the manufacturer's list price to wholesalers before discounts, rebates or other adjustments.

Expected format and rules
- Data type: numeric currency stored as DOUBLE (USD).
- Units: U.S. dollars per unit (e.g., per tablet, vial, bottle) — unit of measure should be inferred from other product fields.
- Value constraints: non‑negative; null when WAC is not available or not applicable.
- Precision/formatting: monetary value with two decimal places when displayed (e.g., 12.34); because the column is a floating point DOUBLE, consumers should format/round to two decimals for presentation to avoid floating‑point artifacts.

Common uses
- Price analysis, gross margin calculations, comparisons versus invoice or net prices.

Column purpose
- AWP (Average Wholesale Price): the unit price used for pricing/payments and analysis (commonly the Average Wholesale Price in pharmaceutical contexts). Represents the listed wholesale price for the product/sku captured in this row.

Expected format and type
- Numeric (DOUBLE in source). Represented as a monetary value in USD per unit or per package as defined by the row’s product/unit columns.
- Non-negative decimal, typically two decimal places (e.g., 12.34). Stored as a floating point number; for financial calculations convert/round to a fixed decimal type (e.g., DECIMAL(10,2)) to avoid precision issues.

Typical constraints and validation
- Must be >= 0; 0 may indicate missing or not applicable when documented elsewhere.
- Nulls allowed if price unavailable; distinguish NULL from 0 by business rules.
- Reasonable ranges depend on product category (e.g., $0.01 – $10,000). Flag extreme outliers for review.
- If price is per package, use accompanying unit_size/package_size columns to normalize to per-unit comparisons.

Usage notes
- Used for reimbursement, pricing analytics, margin calculations, and trend reporting.
- When aggregating or comparing across rows, ensure consistent unit basis (per unit vs per package) and currency normalization if dataset may include other currencies.

Examples
- 5.25 (five dollars and twenty-five cents)
- 0.00 (explicit zero—treat according to business rule)
- NULL (price not available)

Business description
- Meaning: The numeric volumetric amount recorded for the observation/transaction/sample represented by the row. This column holds the magnitude of a volume measurement (for example the volume of product, fluid, sample, or shipment).

Expected format and rules
- Data type/format: floating point number (DOUBLE). Stored as a decimal numeric value (e.g., 12.5, 0.0, 100000.0).
- Units of measure: The numeric value must be interpreted together with the dataset’s unit metadata (a companion column such as VOLUME_UNIT or a catalog-level field). If no separate unit field exists, a canonical unit should be defined in the table documentation (e.g., liters or cubic meters) and all values must use that unit.
- Valid values: non‑negative numbers (>= 0). Null/blank allowed only when volume is unknown or not applicable.
- Precision/scale: allow fractional values; choose precision consistent with measurement needs (typical: up to several decimal places).
- Examples: 0.0 (no volume), 1.25 (one and one quarter in the declared unit), 100000.0 (large volume).

Notes for data consumers
- Always check the associated unit metadata before aggregating or converting values.
- Validate for unreasonable outliers (negative values, extremely large numbers) and convert to a common unit before summarizing across records.

Meaning
- Number of individual items contained in a single package/pack for the product described by the raw_snr_data row (i.e., "units per pack").

Expected format
- Numeric (DOUBLE column). Semantically this should be a non-negative whole number (count), although stored as a double.
- Allowed values: positive integers (1, 2, 6, 12, …). Use NULL when unknown. Zero is normally invalid unless explicitly defined in business rules.

Examples
- 1.0 => single unit pack
- 12.0 => a dozen per pack
- NULL => units-per-pack not provided

Business uses and validation
- Used to convert pack-level measures to unit-level measures (inventory, pricing, sales quantity).
- Downstream systems should cast/round to integer for counts and validate >=1 (or treat NULL as unknown). Flag or correct non-integer or negative values.

Business description (concise)

EXTENDED_UNITS is a numeric measurement value stored as a DOUBLE that represents the sensor/measurement reading expressed in the data source’s “extended” unit scale. It is the raw numeric magnitude associated with the record’s unit type (for example an amplitude, power, flux, or scaled SNR value) and is intended for numeric computations and conversions.

Expected format and rules
- Data type: IEEE 754 double precision (floating point).
- Valid values: any finite real number; use NULL when the extended-unit measurement is missing. NaN/Infinity should be treated as invalid.
- Precision: up to double precision (≈15 decimal digits); store exact decimal values as provided by the instrument.
- Units context: the actual physical unit (e.g., dB, W, V, counts) should be recorded in the companion unit field (e.g., UNITS or UNIT_TYPE). EXTENDED_UNITS holds only the numeric magnitude — do not embed unit labels.
- Typical values/range: sensor-dependent (examples: 0.0, -12.5, 1e3). Consumers should consult the UNIT_TYPE field and device metadata to interpret scale and convert to standard SI units if required.

Usage guidance
- Use for calculations, aggregations and conversions after confirming the associated unit type.
- Validate for finiteness and expected range per device before analytics or alerting.

Business description
- Meaning: The date that identifies the reporting week for the SNR record — i.e., the last calendar day of the reporting week. Each row’s WEEK_ENDING_DATE ties metrics and events to the week that ends on that date (use this column to group, filter and join weekly data).

Expected format and rules
- Data type: DATE (no time component).
- Serialized format: ISO 8601 calendar date, YYYY-MM-DD (for example, 2026-03-07).
- Business rules: must represent the official week boundary as defined by the business (commonly a Saturday or Sunday); values should align to that boundary, be non‑null, not in an unreasonably distant future, and fall within the system’s expected historical range. Duplicate WEEK_ENDING_DATE values are allowed across different entities or metrics but each record’s date must correctly represent its reporting week.

Business description
- What it represents: The SALES_STAGED_FILE_NAME column records the original filename (as landed in the ingestion/staging area) for a sales data file that was ingested into the raw_snr_data bronze table. It is used for traceability, lineage, replay/deduplication, error diagnosis and linking back to the source file or batch.

Expected format and conventions
- General form: a readable, deterministic filename that encodes source and load metadata and includes the file extension.
- Recommended pattern (human- and machine-friendly):
  <source>_<region|env>_<salesType>_<YYYYMMDD>[THHMMSS][_<seq>].<ext>
- Typical components:
  - source: source system or partner identifier (e.g., SAP, POS, PARTNERX)
  - region|env: region or environment tag if applicable (e.g., US, EU, PROD)
  - salesType: business-level type (e.g., ORDERS, RETURNS, INVOICES)
  - timestamp: date or datetime in ISO-ish form (YYYYMMDD or YYYYMMDDTHHMMSS) representing file generation time
  - seq: optional sequence number for multiple files from same timestamp (001, 002)
  - ext: file extension indicating format (csv, json, parquet, csv.gz, json.gz)
- Example filenames:
  - SAP_US_ORDERS_20260301.csv
  - POS_EU_SALES_20260301T153045_001.csv.gz
  - PARTNERX_PROD_RETURNS_20260228.parquet

Validation & storage notes
- Allowed characters: letters, digits, underscores, hyphens and periods (avoid spaces and special characters).
- Case: consistent casing recommended (e.g., upper- or lower-case convention across pipeline).
- Max length: keep ≤ 255 characters to avoid filesystem/metadata issues.
- Nullability: should be populated for every landed file; null indicates missing/malformed ingestion metadata.
- Sensitivity: contains no customer PII — safe to store as descriptive metadata.

Regex (recommended) example
- ^[A-Za-z0-9_-]+_[A-Za-z0-9_-]+_[A-Za-z0-9_-]+_[0-9]{8}(T[0-9]{6})?(_[0-9]{3})?\.(csv|csv\.gz|json|json\.gz|parquet)$

Use this column to map rows back to the physical source file for auditing, reprocessing, and troubleshooting.

Business description
- Meaning: The calendar date when the sales data file was staged (uploaded/placed) into the ingestion/landing area for this raw SNR dataset. This is a file-level metadata field used to identify the file’s business/extract date (i.e., the snapshot date of the staged sales data), not individual transaction timestamps.

Expected format and rules
- Data type: DATE (no time component).
- Format: ISO 8601 date string — YYYY-MM-DD (example: 2026-03-13).
- Constraints/usage: should be a valid calendar date, typically the extract/staging date; used for partitioning, filtering and lineage. If the staging date is unknown, leave NULL per ingestion policy (do not substitute free-form text).

table.table_description:
High-level summary
- This table is the raw (bronze) intake of outlet-level demographic and identifier data for the SNR dataset. It contains one row per recorded outlet/location and captures the outlet’s classification, physical address, regulatory identifier, and source ingestion metadata. It is intended as the landing zone for upstream sources before cleansing and enrichment in higher-level (silver/gold) datasets.

What the data represents (field-by-field)
- REL_ID: Unique record identifier for the row (link/key used to reference this specific outlet record).
- OUTLET_CLASS_OF_TRADE: High-level business type of the outlet (e.g., retail pharmacy, hospital, clinic, wholesaler).
- OUTLET_SUB_CLASS_OF_TRADE: More granular category or subtype of the outlet within the class of trade.
- OUTLET_NAME: Legal or trading name of the outlet/location.
- ADDRESS, CITY, STATE, ZIP_CODE: Physical mailing/location information for the outlet.
- ZIP_SECTOR: Aggregated ZIP-area segment (e.g., 3-digit ZIP prefix or other sectoring used for geospatial grouping).
- DEA_NUMBER: Regulatory identifier (Drug Enforcement Administration number) used for controlled-substance authorization — important for compliance and validation.
- TERRITORY_ID: Sales or operational territory identifier used by commercial/field teams.
- STAGED_FILE_NAME, STAGED_FILE_DATE: Source file name and file ingestion date/time that indicate where and when the record was staged (useful for lineage, troubleshooting, and incremental loads).

Primary uses / business value
- Sales & territory planning: map outlets to territories, identify coverage gaps, plan calls or routing.
- Compliance & verification: validate DEA numbers and addresses for regulated product distribution.
- Reporting & analytics: aggregate counts and trends by outlet class, state, ZIP sector, or territory.
- Master data and enrichment: serve as the raw input for deduplication, address standardization, geocoding, and matching to other master tables (sales, inventory, prescriptions).
- Lineage & auditing: staged file metadata supports traceability back to the source extract.

Important considerations
- Data is in the bronze/raw layer: expect possible duplicates, inconsistent formatting, or missing values. Downstream cleansing/enrichment is recommended before operational use.
- Contains sensitive/regulatory information (DEA_NUMBER) and personal/business contact details (address). Access and use should follow privacy, security, and regulatory controls.
- ZIP_SECTOR semantics may vary by source; confirm definition with the data steward before using for geospatial analysis.

If helpful, I can draft example queries (e.g., outlets per territory, outlets with missing DEA numbers) or a proposed data-quality checklist to promote downstream readiness.

columns.description:
Business description
- REL_ID is the numeric identifier that links a senior’s demographic record to a relationship/relative entity in the system. It is used as a stable foreign key to join this demographic row to detailed relationship or person records (for example to retrieve the related person’s name, contact, or relationship type).

Expected format and rules
- Data type: integer (LONG / BIGINT). 
- Values: positive whole numbers, no decimals, no leading zeros (e.g., 1, 42, 102345). 
- Uniqueness: not necessarily unique in this column by itself (multiple demographic rows can reference the same REL_ID), but it is the unique identifier within the related relationships/persons table. 
- Nulls: allowed only when no related person/relationship exists or is unknown; otherwise must be populated. 
- Persistence: identifier should be stable across snapshots and system updates so joins remain valid.

If a mapping table exists (e.g., relationships or persons), REL_ID should be used to look up human-readable relationship type or PII in that table rather than storing PII in this column.

Meaning
- Identifies the retail trade channel or type/class of the physical or online outlet where sales/customers are associated (e.g., the store format or commercial channel for the location).

Expected format
- Data type: STRING.
- Single categorical value per record (not a list).
- Prefer standardized, enumerated category names (examples): "Supermarket", "Convenience", "Pharmacy", "Mass Merchandiser", "Club", "Specialty", "Discount", "Wholesale", "Online", "Independent", "Franchise".
- Values should be normalized (trimmed, consistent capitalization or all‑upper), no surrounding whitespace or punctuation, and limited to a controlled vocabulary rather than free text.

Notes / usage
- Used for segmentation, reporting, price/promo analysis and channel-level aggregation.
- If unknown or not-applicable, use a consistent sentinel such as NULL or "UNKNOWN".
- Maintain a documented mapping if source systems use different codes (e.g., "GROC" → "Supermarket").

Definition
- The outlet_sub_class_of_trade field records a granular categorical description of an outlet’s commercial format or trade sub-type (a more specific classification than a top‑level "class of trade"). It identifies how the outlet operates and sells goods/services (e.g., convenience store vs. supermarket vs. pharmacy).

Business purpose
- Used to segment customers/outlets, drive channel-specific analysis (sales, pricing, distribution), plan route-to-market strategies, and filter/aggregate performance by outlet format.

Expected format and rules
- Data type: STRING.
- Value style: single categorical label from a standardized, enumerated vocabulary (not free text). Use a canonical name per category, consistently cased (e.g., Title Case or UPPER).
- Maximum length: typically <= 50 characters.
- No multiple values in one field (one sub-class per record); use mapping tables if an outlet belongs to multiple channels.
- Allowed/typical values (examples):
  - Supermarket
  - Hypermarket
  - Convenience Store
  - Independent Grocery / Mom & Pop
  - Discount / Value Retailer
  - Pharmacy / Drugstore
  - Specialty Store
  - Mass Merchandiser
  - Cash & Carry / Wholesale
  - Petrol Forecourt / Forecourt Shop
  - Online / E‑commerce
  - Foodservice / Restaurant
  - Franchise / Chain Store
  - Other
  - Unknown / Not Recorded
- Data quality expectations: values should map to the standardized list; use "Unknown" rather than blanks where classification is unavailable; maintain a lookup table for translations from source terms.

Notes
- Maintain a documented taxonomy (codebook) for the allowed sub-classes and procedures for onboarding new categories to ensure consistent analytics and reporting.

Business definition
- OUTLET_NAME is the human‑readable name of the physical or online location (store, branch, partner site or channel) where the customer interaction, enrollment or transaction was recorded. It is used for reporting, aggregation and joining to outlet/master data to analyze demographics and performance by location.

Expected format and rules
- Type: text/string (expected length up to ~255 characters).
- Content: the official outlet or channel name (e.g., store or branch name, marketplace or channel label). Avoid embedding unrelated fields (IDs, full addresses) in this column.
- Normalization guidance:
  - Trim leading/trailing whitespace and collapse consecutive spaces.
  - Use a consistent casing convention (recommended: Title Case).
  - Standardize common abbreviations (e.g., “St.” → “Street”, “Ctr” → “Center” per corporate naming rules) or follow the company master‑list.
  - Permit letters, numbers, spaces and common punctuation (hyphen, ampersand, apostrophe, period). Avoid control characters.
  - Prefer the canonical name from the outlet master list; if multiple locations share a name, append a disambiguator (e.g., “Walmart Supercenter — Springfield, IL”).
- Privacy: should not contain PII (customer names, account numbers).

Examples
- "CVS Pharmacy"
- "Walmart Supercenter — 1234 Elm St, Springfield"
- "Amazon Marketplace"
- "Acme Bank Branch — Downtown"

Business description
- Meaning: The ADDRESS field contains the physical mailing/location address associated with the demographic record (e.g., the person, household, or entity represented by the SNR record). It is intended to identify where the person or household is located for mailing, service delivery, geocoding, or demographic-analysis purposes.

Expected format and usage guidance
- Data type: free‑form STRING (single field that may contain one or multiple address lines).
- Typical contents: street number and name, unit/apartment/suite (optional), city/locality, state/province/region, postal/ZIP code, and country (when applicable). Examples:
  - "123 Main St Apt 4B, Springfield, IL 62704, USA"
  - "PO Box 456, Toronto, ON M5J 2N1, Canada"
  - "10 Downing St, Westminster, London SW1A 2AA, UK"
- Character set: UTF‑8, may include letters, numbers, spaces, commas, periods, hyphens, and diacritics.
- Normalization: values are typically free‑form but should be standardized where possible (e.g., use postal authority abbreviations for states, consistent placement of postal codes); if address parsing or geocoding will be performed, prefer splitting into separate fields (street_address, unit, city, state, postal_code, country).
- Validation: expect non‑empty, sensible street + locality for production records. Accept PO Box or general delivery entries; flag malformed strings (missing city or postal code) for review.
- Length: should fit within the table STRING limit; for interoperability, keep under ~254 characters.

Privacy and handling
- This column contains personal address (PII). Access, retention, and sharing must comply with applicable privacy policies and regulations (e.g., masking, encryption, and access controls as required).

Business description: CITY is the name of the municipality associated with the demographic record (for example, the individual’s city of residence or the city where the record/event occurred). Expected format: a plain-text city name in natural language (preferably standardized to Title Case), e.g., "Seattle", "New York", "St. Louis", or "San Juan". Allowed characters include letters, spaces, hyphens and apostrophes; do not include state, country or ZIP code in this field (those belong in their own columns). Use NULL/empty when the city is unknown.

Meaning
- The STATE column identifies the U.S. state or territory associated with the demographic record (i.e., the geographic jurisdiction for the person or record).

Expected format
- Use the USPS two‑letter postal abbreviation in uppercase (exactly 2 letters), trimmed, no punctuation or extra whitespace.
- Examples: CA, NY, TX, FL, DC, PR.
- Allowed values include the 50 states plus District of Columbia and U.S. territories commonly represented by USPS codes (e.g., PR, GU, VI, AS).

Data rules / notes
- Null or empty values indicate unknown or not-applicable geography and should be handled explicitly by downstream processes.
- Validate as two uppercase A–Z characters; flag any full state names, lowercase codes, misspellings, or non‑US values for cleaning or mapping.

Business description
- Meaning: Postal ZIP code identifying the U.S. geographic area (postal delivery zone) associated with the demographic record. Used for aggregation, mapping, and joining to external geographic or socioeconomic datasets.

Expected format and usage
- Stored type: LONG (numeric integer).
- Canonical form: 5‑digit U.S. ZIP code (00000–99999) represented as an integer value (no hyphen).
- Display/transfer: When presenting or exporting to users/systems that expect the standard ZIP string, zero‑pad to 5 digits (e.g., database value 501 → "00501").
- Do not expect ZIP+4 in this column; ZIP+4 (9‑digit) values, if present in source data, should be stored in a separate field or truncated/normalized before writing here.

Notes / data quality considerations
- Leading zeros will be lost in the numeric type — ensure formatting when joining or displaying.
- Null or out‑of‑range values indicate missing/unknown location and should be handled in aggregations.
- PO boxes and non‑U.S. codes may appear in source data; validate against an authoritative USPS ZIP list if accuracy is required.

Column name: ZIP_SECTOR

Business description:
- ZIP_SECTOR is a postal-geography identifier that groups addresses into a ZIP-code “sector” used for demographic aggregation and analysis. It identifies a subdivision of a U.S. ZIP code (a ZIP prefix/sector) rather than a full mailing address.
- Although stored as DOUBLE, values are integer-like (no meaningful fractional component). Treat the field as a numeric sector code that should be rendered/used as an integer or zero-padded string for display/reporting.
- Expected values: whole numbers representing the ZIP-sector code (typical formats are 3‑digit or 4‑digit ZIP prefixes depending on the source). Examples: 123.0 (display as "123"), 907.0 (display as "907"). Unknown/missing sectors may be null.
- Usage notes: use for grouping, joins to reference geography tables, and aggregation. When presenting to users, convert to an appropriate fixed-width ZIP-prefix string (pad with leading zeros if your reference data expects them). If exact digit length matters, verify against the source reference table for this dataset.

Business description
- Meaning: DEA_NUMBER is the registrant’s U.S. Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA) registration identifier that uniquely identifies a practitioner, pharmacy, hospital, or other entity authorized to prescribe, dispense, or handle controlled substances.
- Usage: used for validation, matching prescription/controlled‑substance records and compliance/audit workflows.

Expected format
- 9 characters, alphanumeric, typically stored uppercase with no spaces or punctuation.
- Pattern: two letters followed by seven digits (first letter = registrant type code; second = first letter of last name or a number for business registrations).
- Simple regex: ^[A-Z][A-Z0-9]\d{7}$
- Example: AB1234567

Notes
- Values should be normalized (trim whitespace, uppercase) and, where possible, validated using DEA checksum rules. Treat as sensitive/regulatory data and restrict access accordingly.

Business description
- TERRITORY_ID is the canonical identifier for the geographic/sales/operational territory associated with the demographic record. It links each record to the organization’s territory master (used for reporting, routing, manager/owner lookups, and roll-ups by region).

Expected format and rules
- Data type: STRING.
- Semantics: an alphanumeric territory code (not a free-text description); codes map to a territory master table that provides human-readable name, region, manager, etc.
- Typical format: uppercase alphanumeric, may include hyphens or underscores for hierarchy (e.g., region-subregion). Example patterns: US-NW, EU-01, TERR1005, 12345.
- Validation rules: should be non-empty for active records and must match an entry in the territory master registry. Prefer consistent casing (UPPERCASE) and no leading/trailing whitespace. Length is expected to be short (commonly <= 50 characters); adjust to your master list constraints.
- Notes: legacy or vendor-provided values should be mapped to the canonical TERRITORY_ID in the master table before use in analytics or operational workflows.

Business description:
- STAGED_FILE_NAME records the original staging file name from which the demographic record was ingested. It is used for data lineage, auditing, troubleshooting and grouping records by input file.

Expected format:
- A filename (not a full path) including extension, typically containing a source or dataset identifier and a date/timestamp. Recommended readable pattern: <source>_<dataset>_<YYYYMMDD>[ _HHMMSS][ _seq].<ext>
- Allowed characters: letters, digits, underscores, dashes and a single dot before the extension. Examples:
  - snr_demographics_20240215.csv
  - sourceA_snr_20240215_153045_001.json
  - partnerX-export_20240215.parquet

Notes / validation:
- Should not include directory separators (no paths).
- Extension indicates file type (csv, json, parquet, xlsx, etc.).
- Can be null when data is generated or loaded without an originating file.

Definition
- STAGED_FILE_DATE is the calendar date on which the source file containing the record was staged/landed into the Bronze (raw) layer of the pipeline. It represents the file-level staging/ingest day used for lineage, freshness checks, partitioning and incremental loads.

Expected format and type
- Stored as a DATE (date-only, no time or timezone).
- Canonical representation: ISO 8601 date, YYYY-MM-DD (example: 2026-03-13).

Business rules / notes (concise)
- Should normally be populated for every staged record and used to partition or filter ingestion batches.
- Typically corresponds to the file creation or ingest day (day-granularity). If time-of-day is required, use a separate timestamp column (e.g., STAGED_FILE_TIMESTAMP).
- Validation: should not be null for staged data and generally should not be a future date.

table.table_description:
High-level summary
- This table is the raw/staging product master for pharmaceutical products ingested into the intelligence data catalog (bronze layer). It captures product-level attributes and identifiers as they were provided by the source files, plus the source file name and load date.
- Purpose: provide a single place to look up product identity, classification, packaging, strength and manufacturer information so downstream processes (cleansing, matching, pricing, analytics, and reporting) can standardize and enrich product data.

What the data represents (business view)
- Each row represents a single marketed drug product (a specific formulation/packaging of a medicine) as recorded by the source system.
- The table contains identifiers you can use to join to other datasets (for example claims, pricing or inventory), human-readable names and descriptions that help business users understand what the product is, and classification codes used by the source for categorization and grouping.

Column-level, business-friendly descriptions
- MARKET_ID: Identifier for the market or region where the product is sold or reported (useful for market-level segmentation).
- MARKET_NAME: Friendly name of the market or region.
- PRODUCT_ID: Source product identifier (primary key for the product in the source system).
- PRODUCT_NAME: Source product name (may be branded or composite name).
- NDC_11: 11-digit National Drug Code (standard product identifier for drugs in the U.S.) — useful for cross-system matching and regulatory lookups.
- USC_CODE: Source classification code that groups this product into a category used by the data provider.
- USC_DESCRIPTION: Human-readable description of the USC_CODE category.
- BB_USC_CODE: A secondary or alternate classification code provided by the source (if applicable).
- BB_USC_DESCRIPTION: Description for the BB_USC_CODE.
- DRUG_NAME: Common or branded name of the drug product.
- DRUG_GENERIC_NAME: Generic name / active ingredient(s) — useful for therapeutic grouping and substitution analyses.
- FORM_CODE: Code representing dosage form (e.g., tablet, injection).
- FORM_DESCRIPTION: Human-readable form description.
- STRENGTH_DESCRIPTION: Strength or concentration of the product (e.g., “500 mg”).
- PACKAGE_SIZE: Packaging quantity/size (e.g., “30 tablets”).
- MANUFACTURER: Company that manufactures or supplies the product.
- UNIT_OF_MEASURE: Unit used for measuring the product (e.g., tablet, mL).
- STAGED_FILE_NAME: Name of the source file from which this record was ingested (helps traceability).
- STAGED_FILE_DATE: Date (or timestamp) when the source file was staged/loaded.

Common business uses
- Product lookups and cross-references (find product details by NDC or product ID).
- Joining with pricing, claims, inventory or sales data to analyze performance by product, manufacturer, or market.
- Enabling downstream standardization and master data management (deduplication, canonical product IDs).
- Categorization and reporting based on classification codes and therapeutic groupings.
- Data lineage and auditability using staged file name/date for tracing source records.

Data quality notes (what to expect in this bronze layer)
- Records are typically raw as ingested from source files; values may be incomplete, duplicated, or inconsistent and are expected to be normalized in downstream (silver/gold) layers.
- Keys for joins: NDC_11 and PRODUCT_ID are the usual join fields, but confirm uniqueness and cleanliness before using for critical joins.

If you want, I can:
- Provide an example row to illustrate how these fields look in practice.
- Recommend transformations and validation rules to promote this table to a clean master product table for analytics.

columns.description:
- Meaning: Numeric identifier for the market associated with the product. It links each snr_product record to a specific market (e.g., country, trading venue, region or market segment) in your market master/lookup table.

- Expected format and type: 64‑bit integer (LONG). Stored as a whole number with no decimals, punctuation, currency symbols or leading zeros. Positive integers are typical.

- Typical values and usage: Small integer keys such as 1, 10, 100 (or other assigned market IDs). Treated as a foreign key to the market dimension; should be non-null for products that are market-specific and used for joins, filtering and reporting.

- Validation rules (recommended): must be an integer, >0, exist in the market master/lookup table, and consistent across systems.

Business description
- MARKET_NAME identifies the market, region or trading venue associated with the product record. It indicates where the product is sold, distributed or traded (geographic market, market segment, or exchange/venue), not the product name or SKU.

Expected format and rules
- Type: string (text). Max length: typically <= 255 characters.
- Canonicalization: use a standardized set of values (recommended master-market reference table) rather than free text. Prefer consistent casing (e.g., UPPERCASE) and no leading/trailing whitespace.
- Granularity: either a country (ISO-3166 code or full country name), region (e.g., EMEA, APAC), or exchange/venue code (e.g., NYSE, NASDAQ). If needed, combine components with a clear delimiter (recommended: a single hyphen), e.g., US-NYSE or GB-LSE.
- Allowed characters: letters, numbers, spaces, hyphens, slashes or underscores for delimiters; avoid special punctuation.
- Nulls/Unknowns: use a consistent token such as UNKNOWN or NULL; avoid multiple synonyms.

Examples
- United States
- US
- US-NYSE
- NASDAQ
- EMEA
- GLOBAL

Data quality notes
- Map MARKET_NAME values to a canonical market master list for reporting and joins.
- Validate against known market codes to prevent duplicates and synonyms (e.g., UNITED STATES = US).
- Document whether MARKET_NAME refers to legal market, distribution market, or trading venue to avoid ambiguity.

Business description
- PRODUCT_ID is the system-generated, unique numeric identifier for a product record in the SNR product catalog (bronze/raw layer). It serves as the primary key / surrogate key used to join and link product attributes across tables and pipelines.

Expected format and constraints
- Datatype: integer (64-bit long)
- Value: positive whole number (no decimals), typically > 0
- Uniqueness: one-to-one with a product record (no duplicates)
- Stability: immutable once assigned; do not reuse IDs for different products
- Presentation: plain numeric value without leading zeros (examples: 1, 1001, 23984723)
- Nulls: should not be null for canonical product records; handle missing/invalid IDs as data-quality exceptions in the bronze layer

Notes for consumers
- PRODUCT_ID is an internal key for joins and lineage; use human-facing fields such as SKU or product_code for display to users.

Business description
- PRODUCT_NAME is an internal product identifier (product key) used to identify which product a record refers to. Although the column name suggests a textual name, its type is LONG so it stores a numeric product code rather than free‑text.

Expected format and rules
- 64‑bit integer (LONG) — whole numbers only, no decimals.
- Positive, non‑negative values (typical business IDs > 0).
- Must match the product master/catalog key (i.e., foreign key to the product/dimension table) so you can join to retrieve the human‑readable product name and attributes.
- Not intended for descriptive text; do not store product labels or concatenated strings here.
- No leading zeros or formatting characters; store the raw integer value.
- May repeat across rows (many records can reference the same product); uniqueness is not required in this table.

Examples
- 101, 12345, 200001

Notes for consumers
- To show a readable product name in reports, join this column to the product master using the product key. Validate incoming values against the product master to ensure referential integrity.

Meaning
- NDC_11 is the 11‑digit National Drug Code (NDC) that uniquely identifies a specific drug product (labeler, product, package) in U.S. drug/medication datasets. It is used to join and map products to drug master files, claims, pricing, and pharmacy data.

Expected format
- Exactly 11 numeric digits, zero‑padded where necessary (no dashes). Example: 00023456789 or 12345678901.
- Canonical display form (optional) uses the 5-4-2 grouping with dashes: 12345-6789-01.

Notes / data quality considerations
- Stored as DOUBLE can drop leading zeros or introduce floating‑point artifacts; preferred storage is a fixed-length string (CHAR(11)) to preserve leading zeros and ensure exact matching.
- Validate values: length = 11, digits only, and optionally cross-check against an authoritative NDC master list.

I don’t have the project documentation, so below is a concise, practical description plus format rules and recommendations you can validate against your business rules.

- Business meaning (concise): USC_CODE is a numeric identifier for the product used by the organization’s USC classification/lookup system (e.g., a company-specific product code, customs/classification code, or external system reference). It identifies the product record in downstream/legacy systems and is used for joins, reporting and classification.

- Expected format:
  - Stored type: LONG (integer).
  - Values: non-negative whole numbers only (no decimals). Typical length is 4–10 digits depending on your code scheme.
  - Uniqueness: often unique per product (or unique within a given scope); may be a foreign-key to a master product/lookup table.
  - Note: if the code is semantically fixed-length with leading zeros (e.g., "001234"), storing as LONG will strip leading zeros — if leading zeros are significant, use a string type.

- Suggested validations/constraints:
  - NOT NULL if every product must have a USC_CODE.
  - CHECK value >= 0 and within expected max (e.g., <= 9,999,999,999).
  - Foreign-key constraint to the USC master/lookup table if applicable.
  - Audit or lookup mapping if the meaning is an external standard (customs/HTS/other).

If you tell me what “USC” stands for in your business (e.g., Universal Stock Code, U.S. customs code, supplier code), I can make this description exact and provide example values.

Column: USC_DESCRIPTION (STRING)

Business description:
- A human-readable product description for the SNR product catalog. Summarizes the item (brand, model, size/quantity, key attributes) used for display, search, and basic matching.
- Expected format: plain text string (UTF-8), short free-form sentence or phrase, typical length up to 512 characters. Avoid structured markup (no HTML/JSON).
- Content guidelines: include brand/model, unit of measure and quantity where applicable, important attributes (color, material, capacity). Do not include pricing, proprietary PII, or internal codes.
- Normalization/validation: trim leading/trailing whitespace, collapse excessive internal whitespace, remove control characters and unescaped newlines; use consistent casing (sentence or title case) per UI conventions.
- Null/unknown: use NULL when no description is available; prefer concise, meaningful text over long product manuals.

Examples:
- "Acme 12 oz Stainless Steel Travel Mug, Vacuum Insulated, Black"
- "WidgetCorp XC-2000 High-Torque Motor, 240 V, 2 HP"

Business description:
- BB_USC_CODE is the numeric identifier assigned to the security/product by Bloomberg (the Bloomberg Universal/Unique Security Code). It serves as a stable, system-level key used to identify and join security records across pricing, reference and master-data feeds.

Expected format and constraints:
- Data type: integer (LONG).  
- Format: positive whole number, no decimals or formatting characters (e.g., 123456789 or 9876543210).  
- Range: fits a 64‑bit integer (up to ~19 digits).  
- Cardinality: unique per security/product within Bloomberg’s universe; immutable while the security exists.  
- Nulls: may be null for records that are not Bloomberg-sourced or for aggregated/placeholder products.  
- Usage: use this value to join to Bloomberg security master or other tables; do not treat it as a human-readable ticker—map via reference tables to obtain ticker, ISIN, name, etc.

Definition
- A human-readable vendor-supplied description or display label for the product/security represented in this record. It is intended to explain what the BB_USC (security/product identifier) refers to in business terms.

Expected format
- Data type: UTF-8 string.
- Typical length: 1–255 characters (trimmed).
- Content: short descriptive name that may include issuer or product name, instrument type, identifiers/ticker, key attributes (coupon, maturity, contract month), currency, and common punctuation. Avoid control characters and non-printable bytes.
- Case/style: free text (prefer title case or consistent casing per downstream reporting standards).
- Cardinality: usually one descriptive label per security; may be nullable if no vendor description is available.

Examples
- "APPLE INC - COMMON STOCK (AAPL US)"
- "US TREASURY NOTE 2.50% 2028-11-15"
- "CRUDE OIL FUTURE, DEC 2024 (CLZ24)"

Usage notes
- Used for display, reporting, and user-friendly joins; do not rely on this field for programmatic identification (use canonical identifiers like ISIN, CUSIP or BB_USC). Validate/normalize for downstream reporting if consistent formatting is required.

Meaning
- DRUG_NAME contains the name of the pharmaceutical product associated with the product record. It identifies the active ingredient(s) or commercial brand used in safety, cataloging, or product-matching processes.

Expected format and rules
- Type: free-text string (up to a typical DB varchar length; treat as 255 chars max unless DB enforces otherwise).
